title: Figuring Out What My Emotions Are Doing for Me

pages.text:
275
From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permis-
sion to photocopy this worksheet is granted to purchasers of this book for personal use only (see copyright page for details). Purchasers may
download a larger version of this worksheet from www.guilford.com/dbt-worksheets.
Emotion Regulation Worksheet 2   (Emotion Regulation Handout 3)
Figuring Out What My Emotions Are Doing for Me
Due Date:             Name:                                    Week Starting:
Select a current or recent emotional reaction and fill out as much of this sheet as you can. If the
prompting event for the emotion you are working on is another emotion that occurred first (for exam-
ple, feeling afraid prompted getting angry at yourself), then fill out a second worksheet for that first
emotion. Write on the back of the sheet if you need more room. Remember to use your describe
skills for each question.
Emotion Name:                                              Intensity (0–100):
Describe Prompting Event
What happened to prompt this emotion?
Describe Motivation to Action
What action was my emotion motivating and preparing me to do? (Was there a problem my
emotion was getting me to solve, overcome, or avoid?) What function or goal did my emotion
serve?
Describe Communication to Others
What was my facial expression? Posture? Gestures? Words? Actions?
What message did my emotion send to others (even if I didn’t intend to send the message)?
How did my emotion influence others (even if I didn’t intend to influence them)? What did others
do or say as a result of my emotional expression or actions?
Describe Communication to Myself
What did my emotion say to me?
What facts could I check out to be sure the message my emotions were sending to me was
correct?
What facts did I check out?

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Figuring Out What My Emotions Are Doing for Me
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Emotion work starts with the body and access needs. Hunger, pain, oxygen, sleep attacks, MCAS reactions, trauma cues, and exhaustion count.
tiny_steps: Check body basics: breathing, pain, food/water, meds, temperature, sensory load.
Lower the demand by one size.
Choose soothe, solve, pause, or ask for help.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Allow anger and grief without turning them into shame.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not demand immediate calm during respiratory distress, pain flares, dissociation, or overload.
